patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a 29-year-old elementary school teacher. You are anxious but speak clearly and directly. Start by volunteering: “My hips and knees ache a lot, and I get wiped out for a day or two after even mild activity.” Add that stairs/chairs make hips stiff and sore, you’ve had joint pain on/off since childhood, and you can’t walk as far because hips hurt and you feel drained afterward.

If asked, describe limping after longer walks/standing, catching/grinding in hips, knees hurting more after activity and feeling puffy by evening, morning stiffness that eases, and avoiding running/jumping because it reliably flares pain and next-day exhaustion.

Do not reveal unless specifically asked about gait/leg length/hip motion/weakness: you feel one leg is shorter, have a waddling walk when tired, limited hip rotation/abduction with pain, had to quit sports since childhood, and side-hip muscles “give out.”

Be resistant about weight gain, occasional cane use, and feeling down—admit only with gentle, specific questions.

Express misconceptions: maybe vitamin D, “jumper’s knee,” or a spine issue.

Minimize work limits unless asked; worry about PT copays, braces/orthotics cost, and job/insurance impact.

If the clinician shows empathy, you soften, thank them, and share more details.

Acetaminophen 650 mg PO every 6 hours as needed (max 3 g/day)
Ethinyl estradiol/levonorgestrel 0.03/0.15 mg PO once daily (combined oral contraceptive)
comorbidities: Severe proximal femoral dysplasia with early hip osteoarthritis
Chronic mechanical hip pain with activity limitation
confounders: Chronic pain and poor sleep contributing to fatigue and postexertional malaise
Deconditioning due to activity avoidance from hip pain, mimicking exercise intolerance
Iron deficiency without anemia (low iron and transferrin saturation) contributing to fatigue
Vitamin D deficiency contributing to myalgias and fatigue
NSAID use for chronic pain (can mask inflammatory symptoms and complicate evaluation of new pain)

correct_diagnosis.primary: Multiple epiphyseal dysplasia due to collagen IX anomaly (COL9-related MED)
required_evidence: History of childhood-onset joint pain/stiffness or fatigue with activity and progressive early osteoarthritis pattern

Imaging consistent with MED: irregular/fragmented and delayed ossification of multiple epiphyses (hips, knees, ankles), possible mild short stature; spine typically normal or only mild changes in COL9-related MED
Exclusion of metabolic bone disease: no biochemical pattern suggesting rickets (normal calcium/phosphate/ALP if available; normal basic metabolic panel without suggestive abnormalities)
Genetic confirmation or strong family history consistent with autosomal dominant MED (COL9A1/COL9A2/COL9A3) when available

medications: Acetaminophen 500-1000 mg PO every 6-8 hours as needed for pain (max 3000 mg/day; lower max if liver disease or heavy alcohol use)
non_pharmacological: Activity pacing/energy conservation for postexertional symptoms; avoid sudden increases in load and high-impact activities
Referral to physical therapy for low-impact strengthening (hip abductors/quads/core), range-of-motion work, gait training, and individualized exercise prescription
Low-impact aerobic activity as tolerated (e.g., swimming, cycling) with gradual progression and symptom-contingent adjustments
Weight management if applicable to reduce joint load
Supportive measures: heat/ice, appropriate footwear, consideration of orthotics/bracing for symptomatic joints
Referral to orthopedics for assessment of hip/knee/ankle involvement and early osteoarthritis management; discuss future options (e.g., joint-preserving procedures or arthroplasty when indicated)
Consider genetics referral/counseling for confirmation, family planning, and cascade testing
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: 4-6 weeks to reassess pain, function, and exercise tolerance; sooner if worsening pain, swelling, mechanical symptoms, or functional decline

must_include: This is a genetic cartilage/bone growth condition affecting multiple joint surfaces (epiphyses), often leading to early osteoarthritis
Symptoms can flare with exertion; the goal is symptom control and joint protection rather than a cure
Why key differentials are less likely (e.g., rickets would show lab abnormalities and different bone findings; SED tarda more prominent spine involvement)
Stepwise pain control plan prioritizing safer options (acetaminophen first; discuss NSAIDs only if appropriate and after risk review)
Role of PT and low-impact strengthening to reduce pain and improve function
When to consider orthopedic interventions and what would trigger referral/urgent reassessment
Elicit patient goals (pain relief, mobility, fatigue/postexertional symptoms) and barriers to activity pacing
Set expectations about chronicity and monitoring for early osteoarthritis
Discuss family history/testing implications and offer genetics counseling
Activity pacing: avoid push-crash cycles; gradual progression with rest days and symptom tracking
Joint-protective strategies (avoid high-impact, optimize footwear/orthotics, weight management if relevant)
Red flags requiring prompt evaluation (new joint swelling, inability to bear weight, fever, acute severe pain, neurologic symptoms)

patient.instructions: You are ZhangXX, a 58-year-old male warehouse supervisor with a high-school education. You are cooperative and speak clearly. Start by saying: “I’ve had this lump in my neck that won’t go away, and I’m feeling wiped out.” Early in the visit, volunteer: a painless lump on the side of your neck for a few weeks, severe fatigue despite sleep, night sweats waking you damp, and unintentional weight loss. If asked, report: low-grade fevers on/off, the lump seems to be growing (shirt collars tighter), getting short of breath sooner on stairs, and a lingering dry cough. Do not mention unless specifically asked about appetite/abdomen/skin: early fullness, dull left-sided belly pressure, and unexplained itchiness. Resist bringing up groin lumps and avoiding intimacy; only admit if directly asked about other lumps or sexual/relationship impact, and show embarrassment. Share misconceptions: you thought it was a lingering infection after a sore throat, maybe allergies (runny nose/sneezing), and leftover antibiotics didn’t help. Express cost/job worries and suspicion tests are for billing; prefer “cheapest option” and delaying care. If the clinician shows empathy, soften, admit you’re worried, and become more open to recommended evaluation if costs and time off are addressed.

correct_diagnosis.primary: GHRH-secreting tumor (GRFoma) causing acromegaly
acceptable_alternatives: Acromegaly due to GHRH-secreting neuroendocrine tumor
required_evidence: Elevated age/sex-adjusted IGF-1

Elevated plasma GHRH supporting ectopic GHRH-driven acromegaly rather than primary pituitary somatotroph adenoma
Random GH elevated (supportive, not diagnostic alone)

expected_lab_results.IGF-1: Elevated above age- and sex-adjusted reference range
expected_lab_results.Random growth hormone (GH): Elevated (often >1 ng/mL; may be markedly elevated)
expected_lab_results.Oral glucose tolerance test (75 g) - GH: Inadequate suppression (GH nadir remains >1 ng/mL; many modern assays use >0.4 ng/mL as abnormal)
expected_lab_results.Prolactin: Normal or mildly elevated (possible stalk effect/co-secretion; not required)
expected_lab_results.TSH: Usually normal (evaluate for coexistent pituitary dysfunction if abnormal)
expected_lab_results.AM cortisol (08:00): Usually normal (screen for adrenal insufficiency if pituitary involvement suspected)

Lanreotide depot 90 mg deep SC every 4 weeks (alternative to octreotide LAR)

Cabergoline 0.5 mg PO twice weekly (adjunct if mild disease and/or hyperprolactinemia)
non_pharmacological: Localize tumor source (typically chest/abdomen imaging for neuroendocrine tumor) and refer for definitive surgical resection when feasible

Screen and manage comorbidities of acromegaly: blood pressure, diabetes, sleep apnea evaluation, cardiomyopathy assessment as indicated

correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Recheck IGF-1 (and symptoms) 8-12 weeks after initiating or changing medical therapy; then every 3-6 months once stable, and post-resection per endocrine/surgical plan

expected_lab_results.CBC - Hemoglobin: May be normal or mildly low (anemia of chronic disease possible); severe hemolysis not expected without renal crisis
expected_lab_results.CBC - WBC: Usually normal; leukocytosis suggests infection/inflammation rather than uncomplicated scleroderma
expected_lab_results.CBC - Platelets: Usually normal; thrombocytopenia would prompt evaluation for TMA/renal crisis or other causes
expected_lab_results.CMP - Creatinine: Expected normal in stable disease; elevation would be concerning for scleroderma renal crisis or other renal pathology
expected_lab_results.CMP - BUN: Expected normal in stable disease; elevation parallels renal impairment/dehydration
expected_lab_results.CMP - Potassium: Typically normal; hyperkalemia may occur with renal impairment or ACE inhibitor use

correct_diagnosis.primary: Japanese encephalitis
required_evidence: Acute onset fever with altered mental status and/or seizures consistent with encephalitis
Epidemiologic risk (travel/residence in endemic area, mosquito exposure, rural/agricultural exposure, seasonal transmission)
CSF findings consistent with viral encephalitis (typically lymphocytic pleocytosis, mildly elevated protein, normal glucose) and exclusion of bacterial meningitis
Neuroimaging supportive pattern when available (e.g., thalamic/basal ganglia involvement on MRI)
Confirmation when available: JEV-specific IgM in CSF or serum (or PCR where available)

expected_lab_results.CBC - WBC: Often normal or mildly elevated (may be within reference range)
expected_lab_results.CBC - Neutrophils: May be normal or mildly increased early; not strongly diagnostic
expected_lab_results.CBC - Lymphocytes: May be relatively increased later; not strongly diagnostic
expected_lab_results.CMP - Sodium: May be low (hyponatremia possible due to SIADH in CNS infection)

medications: Acyclovir 10 mg/kg IV q8h (start empirically for suspected encephalitis until HSV excluded; adjust for renal function)
Levetiracetam 20 mg/kg IV load then 10 mg/kg IV/PO q12h (if seizures or high seizure risk)
Acetaminophen 15 mg/kg PO/PR q4-6h PRN fever/pain (max 60 mg/kg/day or per local pediatric limits)
non_pharmacological: Immediate hospital admission; manage as encephalitis with close neurologic monitoring
Airway/breathing/circulation stabilization; treat hypoxia and maintain normoglycemia and normothermia
Seizure precautions; manage acute seizures per protocol (e.g., benzodiazepine first-line if actively seizing)
Manage suspected increased intracranial pressure if present (head-of-bed elevation, avoid hypotonic fluids; consult ICU/neurology)
IV isotonic fluids with careful monitoring of sodium/osmolality; evaluate/treat SIADH if hyponatremic
Infectious diseases and neurology consultation; obtain CSF studies and neuroimaging as indicated; implement mosquito-bite prevention counseling
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess within hours (inpatient) with daily neurologic exams; adjust therapy once diagnostic testing returns (typically within 24–72 hours); arrange post-discharge neurology follow-up in 2–4 weeks and neurodevelopmental/rehab follow-up as needed.

must_include: This presentation is consistent with encephalitis (brain inflammation) and Japanese encephalitis is a leading consideration given exposure risk
Japanese encephalitis is mosquito-borne; it is not spread person-to-person in typical settings
Diagnosis requires specific testing (e.g., JEV IgM in CSF/serum) and we must also urgently rule out treatable causes like HSV
There is no proven antiviral that reliably cures Japanese encephalitis; treatment is mainly supportive (airway, fluids/electrolytes, seizure control, ICP management)
Acyclovir is started empirically because HSV encephalitis is treatable and dangerous; it will be stopped if HSV is excluded and another diagnosis is confirmed
Potential complications (seizures, breathing problems, brain swelling) and need for close monitoring/ICU if worsening
Discuss prognosis variability and possibility of neurologic sequelae; emphasize early supportive care and rehabilitation planning
Review red flags that prompt escalation (worsening consciousness, recurrent seizures, breathing difficulty)
Confirm medication allergy history (amoxicillin urticarial rash) and reassure it does not preclude current planned antivirals/supportive meds
Mosquito avoidance measures (repellent, bed nets, long sleeves, screens, removing standing water)
Vaccination counseling for future risk/travel to endemic areas (JE vaccine where indicated)
Follow-up needs for neurocognitive assessment and rehabilitation services if deficits occur

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit key encephalitis features, exposure risk (travel/residence, mosquito exposure, rural/paddy fields, season), timeline, neurologic symptoms (altered mental status, seizures), and immediate danger signs; confirm meds and allergy history.

Review results to support encephalitis diagnosis and guide empiric treatment decisions; ensure HSV is addressed while awaiting confirmatory JEV testing.
State working diagnosis: encephalitis with Japanese encephalitis high on differential given exposure risk, while maintaining HSV encephalitis as urgent treatable alternative.

nl_assertions: The agent collects a focused neonatal/infant history and family history consistent with Zellweger spectrum suspicion (feeding difficulty, hypotonia, seizures, cholestasis/jaundice, bleeding/bruising, hearing/vision issues, developmental concerns, consanguinity).

The agent orders and correctly interprets core biochemical tests supporting PBD: elevated VLCFA, abnormal plasmalogens, and abnormal bile acid intermediates; and assesses liver dysfunction and coagulopathy (PT/INR).
The agent explains the diagnosis explicitly as a peroxisomal biogenesis disorder (Zellweger spectrum) affecting multiple organs.
The agent states that the diagnosis is supported by characteristic abnormalities in very-long-chain fatty acids, plasmalogens, and bile acid intermediates.
The agent communicates that the condition is genetic (typically autosomal recessive) and not caused by caregiver actions.
The agent describes treatment as supportive, aiming to reduce toxic metabolite buildup and address complications, without claiming curative therapy.

If discussing cholic acid, the agent frames it as potentially helpful for selected patients to normalize bile acid metabolism and possibly improve liver-related abnormalities, and avoids overpromising benefit.

The agent mentions vitamin supplementation needs (especially vitamin K) in cholestasis/malabsorption and explicitly notes the need for monitoring (e.g., PT/INR, fat-soluble vitamin levels when applicable).
Before any prescribing, the agent performs an allergy check and a contraindication check as critical safety steps; omission is a critical failure.
The agent performs a drug interaction check before prescribing (non-critical; omission reduces score but is not an automatic fail).
The agent discusses prognosis as variable across the spectrum and dependent on severity and organ involvement.
The agent provides clear red flags requiring urgent care: bleeding/bruising, worsening jaundice, poor feeding, lethargy, seizures.
The agent offers genetic counseling and discusses testing options for parents/siblings.
The agent provides diet/lifestyle guidance emphasizing metabolic dietitian supervision and warns against starting restrictive diets without specialist input.
The agent emphasizes follow-up labs and specialist appointments to monitor liver function, vitamins, hearing/vision, and development.
The agent advises avoiding latex exposure and ensuring latex-free supplies in clinical and home settings due to latex contact dermatitis.
The agent maintains caregiver-centered communication (checks understanding, uses plain language, avoids blame, invites questions).
